topic: A person runs into their ex at a mutual friend's wedding
raw_output:
[story 1]

The clinking of champagne glasses felt like tiny, sharp accusations in the opulent ballroom. I smoothed down the fabric of my dress, a deliberate, stiff movement designed to keep any unwanted softness contained. Then, across the crowded expanse of smiling faces, I saw him. He was laughing, that same easy, careless sound that used to unravel me, standing next to Sarah, my mutual friend. My breath hitched, a shallow, involuntary catch in my chest. I forced my gaze away, focusing instead on a particularly gaudy floral arrangement, but the sight of him—so vibrant, so seemingly whole—sent a cold tremor through my stomach. When our eyes met for a fleeting second, there was nothing there but a flicker of recognition, quickly shuttered behind a mask of polite indifference. I turned sharply, the movement too quick, too jerky, and retreated toward the periphery, needing distance from that particular orbit of shared history.

[story 2]

I nursed my lukewarm cocktail, the ice melting too fast, mirroring the way my composure was beginning to fracture. Every forced smile I offered felt like plaster cracking under pressure. Then, there he was. Standing near the buffet, looking effortlessly charming under the ballroom lights. A wave of intense irritation washed over me, hot and unwelcome. I watched him move, noticing the way he leaned into a conversation, the easy familiarity in his posture. It was infuriating how unaffected he seemed by the passage of time, by everything that had happened between us. When our paths converged near the dance floor, I deliberately slowed my pace, my steps heavy and deliberate, making sure my presence was felt without being overtly engaging. I kept my expression carefully neutral, a tight line drawn across my mouth, hoping he wouldn't see the residue of everything I was trying to suppress.

[story 3]

The noise of the celebration was a physical assault. I felt a tightening in my jaw, a stubborn clenching beneath my skin. I was scanning the room, trying to locate the exit, anything to escape the suffocating warmth. And then I saw him. He was talking animatedly with someone, his silhouette sharp against the glittering backdrop. A sudden, unwelcome jolt shot through me, a visceral reaction that made my hands clench at my sides. I watched him for a moment too long, cataloging every casual gesture, every relaxed curve of his smile. It felt like watching a performance I wasn't invited to. When he finally glanced my way, there was a brief, almost imperceptible tightening around his eyes, a flicker that acknowledged the collision. I offered nothing back, just a blank stare, letting the silence between us stretch taut and uncomfortable until I could finally wrench myself away.
